content.text: hello

content.thinking:
**Formulating a greeting**

I see that I need to craft a greeting. It’s important to make it friendly and welcoming to set the right tone from the beginning! Maybe I could say something simple like, “Hello! How can I assist you today?” or something more personalized if I know the user’s name. I want to ensure it feels warm and inviting, so the user feels comfortable engaging with me. Alright, let’s put my best foot forward!

content.text: Hello! What can I help you with?

can you make a global pi extension that renders assisstant tool calls as just a single line. if it's too long, just truncate it at the end with "...". DO NOT apply this for code edits. but do it for everything else (read, bash, etc.)

Title: packages/coding-agent/README.md at main · earendil-works/pi
URL: https://github.com/earendil-works/pi/blob/main/packages/coding-agent/README.md
Published: N/A
Author: N/A
Highlights:
Then just talk to pi. By default, pi gives the model four tools: `read`, `write`, `edit`, and `bash`. The model uses these to fulfill your requests. Add capabilities via skills, prompt templates, extensions, or pi packages.
[...]
For each built-in provider, pi maintains a list of tool-capable models, updated with every release. Authenticate via subscription (`/login`) or API key, then select any model from that provider via `/model` (or Ctrl+L).
[...]
| Feature | How |
|---------|-----|
| File reference | Type `@` to fuzzy-search project files |
| Path completion | Tab to complete paths |
| Multi-line | Shift+Enter (or Ctrl+Enter on Windows Terminal) |
| Images | Ctrl+V to paste (Alt+V on Windows), or drag onto terminal |
| Bash commands | `!command` runs and sends output to LLM, `!!command` runs without sending |
[...]
### Tool Options
[...]
| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `--tools `, `-t ` | Allowlist specific tool names across built-in, extension, and custom tools |
| `--exclude-tools `, `-xt ` | Disable specific tool names across built-in, extension, and custom tools |
| `--no-builtin-tools`, `-nbt` | Disable built-in tools by default but keep extension/custom tools enabled |
| `--no-tools`, `-nt` | Disable all tools by default |
[...]
Available built-in tools: `read`, `bash`, `edit`, `write`, `grep`, `find`, `ls`
[...]
# Read-only mode
pi --tools read,grep,find,ls -p "Review the code"

---

Title: Built-in Tools
URL: https://badlogic-pi-mono.mintlify.app/api/coding-agent/tools
Published: N/A
Author: N/A
Highlights:
# Built-in Tools
[...]
> File system and shell tools provided by Pi
[...]
Pi provides a set of built-in tools for file system operations and shell command execution. These tools are available to the agent by default.
[...]
```typescript
import {
  readTool,
  writeTool,
  editTool,
  bashTool,
  grepTool,
  findTool,
  lsTool,
  codingTools,
} from "@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent";

```
[...]
## Tool Factories
[...]
All tools are created using
[...]
functions that accept a working directory and optional configuration.
[...]

### createGrepTool
[...]
### createFindTool
[...]
by name pattern

### createLsTool
[...]
## Pre-built Tool Sets
[...]
### codingTools
[...]
All tools for full file system access.
[...]
Includes: `read`, `write`, `edit`, `bash`, `grep`, `find`, `ls`
[...]
### createCodingTools
[...]
### readOnlyTools
[...]
Includes: `read`, `grep`, `find`, `ls`

### createReadOnlyTools
[...]
## Example: Custom Tool Set
[...]
```typescript

  createReadTool,
  createWriteTool,
  createBashTool,
} from "@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent";
[...]
const tools = [

cwd),

: (context) => {
      console.log(`Executing: ${context

];

Title: packages/coding-agent/docs/usage.md at main · earendil-works/pi
URL: https://github.com/earendil-works/pi/blob/main/packages/coding-agent/docs/usage.md
Published: N/A
Author: N/A
Highlights:
### Tool Options
[...]
| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `--tools `, `-t ` | Allowlist specific built-in, extension, and custom tools |
| `--no-builtin-tools`, `-nbt` | Disable built-in tools but keep extension/custom tools enabled |
| `--no-tools`, `-nt` | Disable all tools |
[...]
Built-in tools: `read`, `bash`, `edit`, `write`, `grep`, `find`, `ls`.
[...]
# Read-only mode
pi --tools read,grep,find,ls -p "Review the code"

---

Title: Overview - Pi
URL: https://badlogic-pi-mono.mintlify.app/coding-agent/overview
Published: N/A
Author: N/A
Highlights:
> ## Documentation Index
> 
> Fetch the complete documentation index at: https://mintlify.com/badlogic/pi-mono/llms.txt
> Use this file to discover all available pages before exploring further.
[...]
### Built-in Tools
[...]
By default, pi gives the model four essential tools:
[...]
- `read` - Read file contents
- `write` - Create new files
- `edit` - Modify existing files with precise replacements
- `bash` - Execute shell commands
[...]
Optional tools available via `--tools` flag:
[...]
- `grep` - Search file contents
- `find` - Find files by pattern
- `ls` - List directory contents
[...]
## Example Usage
[...]
```bash
# One-shot response
pi -p "Summarize this codebase"
[...]
# With file context
pi -p @screenshot.png "What's in this image?"

# Read-only mode
pi --tools read,grep,find,ls -p "Review the code"

```

---

content.thinking:
Now I have a clear picture. The built-in tools are: read, bash, edit, write, grep, find, ls. I also see there are web tools: web_search, code_search, fetch_content, get_search_content.

Wait, looking at the user's original message, they mentioned "read, bash, etc." and "DO NOT apply this for code edits". The web_search, code_search etc. are also tools. Let me check if those are built-in or from some other package.

Actually, from the context of the conversation, the user is talking about the tools I have access to in this conversation - read, bash, edit, write, web_search, code_search, fetch_content, get_search_content. Those are the tools available to me as an AI assistant.

But the user wants a pi extension. So they're talking about pi's built-in tools. The built-in tools in pi are read, bash, edit, write, grep, find, ls. The web_search, code_search etc. are tools that I (the AI) have, not tools that pi has.

Wait, but looking at the conversation, the user is a human talking to me, and they're asking me to create a pi extension. So the extension would be for pi's TUI, to render pi's assistant tool calls.

Let me re-read the request: "can you make a global pi extension that renders assistant tool calls as just a single line. if it's too long, just truncate it at the end with '...'. DO NOT apply this for code edits. but do it for everything else (read, bash, etc.)"

So they want all tool calls in pi's TUI to render as a single line, truncated if too long, except for `edit`. 

Looking at the `built-in-tool-renderer.ts` example, I need to override each tool's `renderCall` and `renderResult` to be a single line. But I need to be careful - the user wants the ENTIRE tool call (both call and result) to be a single line? Or just the call part?

Looking at the example, `renderCall` is what shows when the tool is invoked, and `renderResult` shows after execution. The user said "renders assistant tool calls as just a single line" - this likely means the `renderCall` part should be a single line. But they might also want the result to be compact.

Actually, re-reading more carefully: "renders assistant tool calls as just a single line" - I think they mean the visual representation of the tool call in the TUI. When pi shows a tool being called, it currently shows multiple lines with arguments. The user wants this compacted to one line.

But wait - looking at `renderShell: "self"` - when a tool uses `renderShell: "self"`, it renders its own outer shell. Without it, the default `Box` handles padding and background. The `renderCall` and `renderResult` are inside that box.

If I want a truly single-line display, I might need to use `renderShell: "self"` and return a single-line `Text` component. But actually, looking at the default behavior, the tool call and result are separate components stacked vertically. 

Actually, I think what the user wants is simpler - they want `renderCall` to just show the tool name + a compact summary on one line, and `renderResult` to also be a single line. The default Box will still wrap it, but the content itself will be one line.

Let me look at what the built-in tool renderer example does - it already makes them fairly compact. But I can make them even more compact - just a single line.

For `renderCall`, I'll make each tool show:
- `read <path>` 
- `$ <command>` for bash
- `write <path>` 
- `grep <pattern>` etc.

For `renderResult`, a single line status:
- "✓ <line count> lines" for read
- "✓ exit 0" for bash
- etc.

And truncate the call line if it's too long.

Wait, but I also need to handle the fact that some tools might not be built-in but could be registered by other extensions. The user said "for everything else" - so I should probably also handle any custom tools that might be registered.

Hmm, but I can only override tools I know about. For built-in tools I can re-register them. For extension tools, I can't easily intercept them unless I know their names.

Actually, there's a better approach. Instead of overriding every single tool, I can look at how pi renders tool calls. Looking at the TUI component for tool execution, there might be a way to override the rendering more globally. But the docs say:

"If a slot renderer is not defined or throws:
- renderCall: Shows the tool name
- renderResult: Shows raw text from content"

And "Built-in renderer inheritance is resolved per slot. Execution override and rendering override are independent."

So the cleanest approach is to override each built-in tool except `edit`. For any extension tools, they would need their own renderers.

Actually wait - I can also look at `renderShell: "self"` on all tools except edit. When a tool returns a single-line `Text` component, it's rendered in a box by default. With `renderShell: "self"`, the tool controls its own framing.

But the user just said "renders assistant tool calls as just a single line" - I think they mean the content inside the box should be one line, not necessarily removing the box. The default box is fine.

Let me re-read the request once more: "renders assistant tool calls as just a single line. if it's too long, just truncate it at the end with '...'. DO NOT apply this for code edits. but do it for everything else (read, bash, etc.)"

I think they want:
1. The `renderCall` output to be a single line (tool name + args, truncated)
2. The `renderResult` output to also be a single line (status, truncated)

Actually, maybe they only care about the call part? Or both? Let me interpret it as both call and result should be compact single lines.

But wait - there's a subtlety. The `edit` tool should NOT be compacted. So for `edit`, I should not override the renderer at all, letting the built-in diff rendering stay as-is.

For all other tools, I override with compact single-line renderers.

Now, which tools should I handle?
Built-in: read, bash, write, grep, find, ls
I should also consider web tools if pi has them. But from the docs, pi's built-in tools are just read, bash, edit, write, grep, find, ls.

Actually, looking at the examples and my own tool list in the conversation, I see `web_search`, `code_search`, `fetch_content`, `get_search_content`. But those are MY tools as an AI assistant, not pi's tools. The user is asking about a pi extension for pi's TUI.

Wait, but maybe pi does have web tools? Let me check if there are any other tool factories.

content.thinking:
OK so those web tools are not part of pi's built-in tools. They're my tools as the AI assistant. The user wants a pi extension that affects how pi renders its assistant's tool calls.

So I need to override: read, bash, write, grep, find, ls
And NOT override: edit

Let me create the extension. I'll put it in `~/.pi/agent/extensions/compact-tool-renderer.ts`.

For the single-line format:
- read: `read <path>` (truncate path if too long)
- bash: `$ <command>` (truncate command if too long)  
- write: `write <path>` (truncate)
- grep: `grep <pattern>` (truncate)
- find: `find <pattern>` (truncate)
- ls: `ls <path>` (truncate)

For results:
- read: `<line count> lines` or `error`
- bash: `exit <code>` or `done`
- write: `written`
- grep: `<count> matches`
- find: `<count> files`
- ls: `<count> items`

All single line, truncated with "..." if too long.

Let me also think about what the user means by "too long". I'll set a max width, maybe something like terminal width or a fixed number. But in the renderer, I don't have terminal width directly. I could just set a reasonable max like 120 chars and truncate.

Actually, looking at the `renderCall` and `renderResult` functions, they return a `Text` component. The `Text` component will be displayed. If the text is too long for the terminal width, the TUI will handle wrapping. But the user specifically said "if it's too long, just truncate it at the end with '...'".

So I should manually truncate strings. I'll use a max length like 100 or 120 characters.

Wait, I should check if pi's TUI framework handles truncation automatically or if I need to do it myself. The user explicitly asked me to truncate with "...", so I'll do it manually.

Let me look at how the `built-in-tool-renderer.ts` example does truncation:
```typescript
const cmd = args.command.length > 80 ? `${args.command.slice(0, 77)}...` : args.command;
```

I'll follow this pattern but maybe use a consistent max length.

Now let me think about the edit tool. The user said DO NOT apply this for code edits. So I should just skip overriding the edit tool entirely.

But wait - there's a subtle issue. If I override `renderCall` for edit but keep the default `renderResult`, that would partially affect edit. The user said don't apply the single-line rendering for code edits. So I should not override edit at all.

Let me write the extension.

For the imports, I need:
- `ExtensionAPI` from `@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent`
- `createReadTool`, `createBashTool`, `createWriteTool`, `createGrepTool`, `createFindTool`, `createLsTool` from `@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent`
- `Text` from `@earendil-works/pi-tui`
- Various detail types

Looking at the example, the detail types are: `BashToolDetails`, `ReadToolDetails`, etc.

Let me also check if there are details types for grep, find, ls.

Good, the directory exists. Now let me write the extension. Let me also think about whether I should use `renderShell: "self"` to make the tool display even more compact.

Actually, without `renderShell: "self"`, the default ToolExecutionComponent wraps each slot in a Box with padding. So a tool call would look like:

┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│ read path/to/file                   │
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│ 42 lines                            │
└─────────────────────────────────────┘

With `renderShell: "self"`, I could make it:
│ read path/to/file → 42 lines       │

But that's more complex because I'd need to handle both call and result in one component, and I don't think `renderShell` merges the slots - it just replaces the outer box.

Actually, looking at the docs again: "renderShell: 'self' when the tool should render its own shell instead of using the default Box." So each slot (renderCall and renderResult) would be responsible for its own shell. But they would still be separate rows.

I think the user is fine with two lines (call + result) as long as each is a single line. The default Box is actually nice for visual separation. I'll keep it.

Let me write the extension now.

content.text:
Done. The extension is written to:

`~/.pi/agent/extensions/compact-tool-renderer.ts`

Run `/reload` in pi to activate it. Here's what it does:

- **Read** → `read path/to/file (offset=N, limit=M)` → `42 lines`
- **Bash** → `$ command` → `done (12 lines)` or `exit 1 (5 lines)`
- **Write** → `write path/to/file (20 lines)` → `Written`
- **Grep** → `grep pattern in path` → `7 matches`
- **Find** → `find pattern in path` → `3 files`
- **Ls** → `ls path` → `15 items`

Everything is capped at 120 chars and truncated with `...` if it exceeds that. The `edit` tool is left untouched so diffs still render fully.
